analogy: Think of "Kinship Caste and Class" through the chapter's own opening images: 53 In the previous chapter we saw that there were several changes in economic and political life between c. Some of these changes influenced societies as well. For instance, the extension of agriculture into forested areas transformed the lives of forest dwellers; craft specialists often... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Kinship Caste and Class is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that 53 In the previous chapter we saw that there were several changes in economic and political life between c.

The text explains that some of these changes influenced societies as well.

The text explains that for instance, the extension of agriculture into forested areas transformed the lives of forest dwellers; craft specialists often emerged as distinct social groups; the unequal distribution of wealth sharpened social differences.

The text explains that historians often use textual traditions to understand these processes.

The text explains that some texts lay down norms of social behaviour; others describe and occasionally comment on a wide range of social situations and practices.

The text explains that we can also catch a glimpse of some social actors from inscriptions.

The text explains that as we will see, each text (and inscription) was written from the perspective of specific social categories.

The text explains that so we need to keep in mind who composed what and for whom.

The text explains that we also need to consider the language used, and the ways in which the text circulated.

The text explains that used carefully, texts allow us to piece together attitudes and practices that shaped social histories.

The text explains that in focusing on the Mahabharata, a colossal epic running in its present form into over 100,000 verses with depictions of a wide range of social categories and situations, we draw on one of the richest texts of the subcontinent.

The text explains that it was composed over a period of about 1,000 years (c.

The text explains that 500 BCE onwards), and some of the stories it contains may have been in circulation even earlier.

The text explains that the central story is about two sets of warring cousins.

The text explains that the text also contains sections laying down norms of behaviour for various social groups.

The text explains that occasionally (though not always), the principal characters seem to follow these norms.

The text explains that often people belonging to the same family share food and other resources, and live, work and perform rituals together.

The text explains that under patriliny, sons could claim the resources (including the throne in the case of kings) of their fathers when the latter died.

The text explains that source 1 Sketch map not to scale KURU Indraprastha SHURASENA Mathura Virata MATSYA Ujjayini AVANTI Hastinapura VATSA Kaushambi KOSHALA Shravasti Ayodhya Sarnath Varanasi Bodh Gaya Kushinagara MALLA SAKYA Lumbini Kapilavastu Pava Vaishali Ganga Yamuna Map 1 The Kuru Panchala region and neighbouring areas Pataliputra

The text explains that they had no claims to the resources of the household.

The text explains that source 2 KINSHIP, CASTE AND CLASS Ü Read the passage and list the different criteria suggested for becoming king.

The text explains that source 3 Ü For each of the forms, discuss whether the decision about the marriage was taken by (a) the bride, (b) the bridegroom, (c) the father of the bride, (d) the father of the bridegroom, (e) any other person.

The text explains that source 4 Ü How many Gotami-putas and how many Vasithi (alternative spelling Vasathi)-putas are there?

The text explains that source 7 “Proper” social roles Here is a story from the Adi Parvan of the Mahabharata: Once Drona, a Brahmana who taught archery to the Kuru princes, was approached by Ekalavya, a forest- dwelling nishada (a hunting community).

The text explains that in fact, political power was effectively open to anyone who could muster support and resources, and rarely depended on birth as a Kshatriya.

The text explains that source 8 Ü Do you think the silk weavers were following the occupation laid down for them in the Shastras?

The text explains that this rested on a notion that certain activities, especially those connected with the performance of rituals, were sacred and by Source 9 Ü Identify the practices described in this passage which seem non-Brahmanical.

The text explains that which of the sources mentioned in this section suggest that people followed the occupations laid down by Brahmanas?

The text explains that he asked her to take a bit of the leftover from his bowl and give it to Mandavya and the Brahmanas … Source 10 Ü Identify elements in the story that suggest that it was written from the perspective of Matanga.

The text explains that beyond Birth Resources and Status If you recall the economic relations discussed in Chapter 2, you will realise that slaves, landless agricultural labourers, hunters, fisherfolk, pastoralists, peasants, village headmen, craftspersons, merchants and kings emerged as social actors in different parts of the subcontinent.

The text explains that their social positions were often shaped by their access to economic resources.

The text explains that here we will examine the social implications of access to resources in certain specific situations.

The text explains that issues of ownership, foregrounded in stories such as this one (Source 11), also figure in the Dharmasutras and Dharmashastras.

The text explains that women could not claim a share of these resources.

The text explains that however, cumulative evidence – both epigraphic and textual – suggests that while upper-class women may have had access to resources, land, cattle and money were generally controlled by men.

The text explains that in other words, social differences between men and women were sharpened because of the differences in access to resources.

The text explains that source 11 Ü Do you think that this episode suggests that wives could be treated as the property of their husbands?

The text explains that source 12 KINSHIP, CASTE AND CLASS Ü Compare and contrast the ways in which men and women could acquire wealth.

The text explains that poems included in the Tamil Sangam anthologies often illuminate social and economic Source 13 Ü Read Avantiputta’s first statement again.

The text explains that are there any similarities or differences with patterns of the past? relationships, suggesting that while there were differences between rich and poor, those who controlled resources were also expected to share them.

The text explains that if we request him, showing him our ribs thin with hunger, he will go to the blacksmith of his village And will say to that man of powerful hands: “Shape me a long spear for war, one that has a straight blade!” Source 14 Ü What are the strategies which the bard uses to try and persuade the chief to be generous?

The text explains that handling Texts Historians and the Mahabharata If you look through the sources cited in this chapter once more you will notice that historians consider several elements when they analyse texts.

The text explains that source 15 Source 16 Ü Do you think Lal’s finds match the description of Hastinapura in the epic? Ü Why do you think the author(s) offered three explanations for a single episode?

The text explains that at the same time, the fact that so many different explanations are offered for the episode (Source 16) suggests that polyandry gradually fell into disfavour amongst the Brahmanas, who reworked and developed the text through the centuries.

The text explains that some early sources suggest that polyandry was not the only or even the most prevalent form of marriage.

The text explains that the sources that historians use to reconstruct this exciting world of ideas and beliefs include Buddhist, Jaina and Brahmanical texts, as well as a large and impressive body of material remains including monuments and inscriptions.

The text explains that while we will be focusing on Buddhism, it is important to remember that this tradition did not develop in isolation – there were several other traditions, each engaged in debates and dialogues with the others.

The text explains that among the best preserved monuments of the time is the stupa at Sanchi which is a major focus in this chapter.

The text explains that a Glimpse of Sanchi Sanchi in the nineteenth century The most wonderful ancient buildings in the state of Bhopal are at Sanchi Kanakhera, a small village under the brow of a hill some 20 miles north-east of Bhopal which we visited yesterday.

The text explains that we inspected the stone sculptures and statues of the Buddha and an ancient gateway … The ruins appear to be the object of great interest to European gentlemen.

The text explains that major Alexander Cunningham … stayed several weeks in this neighbourhood and examined these ruins most carefully.

The text explains that he took drawings of the place, deciphered the inscription, and bored shafts down these domes.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History I, Chapter 3: Kinship Caste and Class

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEME and THREE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Kinship Caste and Class; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Kinship Caste and Class through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THEME and THREE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
